era_context: Fourth Dynasty Egypt under Khufu; Great Pyramid of Giza in construction. Mesopotamia in Early Dynastic Period III. Indus Valley in Early Harappan phase. This year represents the height of Old Kingdom Egyptian power and monumental achievement.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia beyond the Indus Valley

events.title: Great Pyramid of Giza construction in progress under Khufu
events.description: The Great Pyramid continues under construction during Khufu's reign. The project involves the quarrying of limestone blocks from the Giza Plateau and surrounding areas, their transport to the construction site, and their placement with remarkable precision. The pyramid's construction represents an unprecedented mobilisation of labour and resources. By 2564 BCE, the pyramid is in its mid-construction phases, with the internal chambers and passages being constructed or completed.
events.certainty_note: The pyramid's existence and Khufu's attribution are archaeologically confirmed. The precise year of construction is uncertain (±5–10 years).

events.title: Egyptian state administration and labour mobilisation for monumental construction
events.description: The construction of the Great Pyramid requires the mobilisation of vast labour forces, quarrying operations, and supply chains. The state controls the distribution of food, tools, and materials. Workers are housed in temporary settlements near the construction site. The project demonstrates the state's capacity to extract resources from the broader economy and concentrate them on a single monumental goal. This mobilisation is supported by a sophisticated administrative system and ideological legitimacy rooted in the pharaoh's divine status.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza, including worker settlements

events.certainty_note: The existence of labour mobilisation is confirmed by archaeological evidence. Specific details for 2564 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Part of broader Old Kingdom economic system

events.title: Mesopotamian Early Dynastic Period III: city-state conflicts and irrigation agriculture
events.description: In southern Mesopotamia, city-states compete for control of irrigation resources and territory. Lagash, Uruk, and Ur are major centres. Irrigation agriculture supports dense urban populations. Conflicts over water rights are common. Cuneiform writing is used for administrative and commercial purposes. Trade networks extend to the Persian Gulf and beyond.
key_figures: Rulers of Lagash, Uruk, and Ur (names and dates uncertain)
events.certainty_note: The ED III period is confirmed archaeologically. Specific events for 2564 BCE are not documented. Mesopotamian chronology remains uncertain.
cross_references: Part of Early Dynastic Period III (c. 2600–2334 BCE)

events.title: Indus Valley Early Harappan phase: settlement expansion and standardisation
events.description: The Early Harappan phase continues, with settlements expanding and showing increasing standardisation in pottery, tools, and settlement patterns. Trade connections with Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf are evident. The proto-urban centres are developing the social and economic structures that will characterise the Mature Harappan phase.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The Early Harappan phase is archaeologically confirmed. Specific events for 2564 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Part of Early Harappan phase (c. 3300–2600 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The chronological uncertainties noted for previous years apply equally to 2564 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2564 BCE is similar to that for 2560–2563 BCE. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward Egypt and monumental contexts. The lives of ordinary people and non-elite settlements are largely invisible. The five-year span of 2560–2564 BCE shows remarkable continuity in the major civilisations: Egypt's pyramid construction, Mesopotamian city-state competition, and Indus Valley settlement development all continue without documented major disruptions.

graph_edges.note: Indus Valley Early Harappan development continues
